coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and sulphur co-doped carbon quantum dot integrated bismuth oxide nanocomposite for photocatalytic degradation and electrochemical sensing applications
coredata.dc:creator: John, Bony K.
Mathew, Beena
coredata.dc:description: This study developed a novel nitrogen and sulphur co-doped carbon quantum dot-modified bismuth oxide binary composite (N,S-CQD- Bi2O3) through a hydrothermal method. The binary composite was successfully evaluated for its solar light-assisted photocatalytic abatement of toxic refractory pollutants such as dyes (rhodamine B, xylenol orange and sunset yellow), pesticides (2,4-D and diuron) and pharmaceutical drug (tetracycline). It was also successfully applied for the electrochemical detection of aniline molecules. The structural, morphological, optical, and electrochemical properties of the developed photocatalyst were investigated by XRD, FESEM, TEM, UV–vis DRS, XPS, PL, CV, amperometry and impedance techniques. Among the three different weight percentage binary composites, the 5% N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 displayed maximum photocatalytic performance. In all cases, the binary heterojunction exhibited a degradation efficiency of over 90% by converting the harmful compounds into less harmful ones. The scavenger experiments showed the roles of ˙OH and O2 −˙ radicals in the photodegradation. The improved photocatalytic behaviour was ascribed to the Z-scheme heterojunction formation, enhancements in visible light absorption, improved surface morphology and efficient separation and transfer of electron-hole pairs. The electrochemical detection method yields a LOD of 0.25 nM within the linear range of 5–35 nM. Real sample analysis of aniline also produces excellent results with outstanding reproducibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Bismuth nitrate
Photocatalyst
Electrochemistry
Rhodamine B
Aniline

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic activity of the N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 composite
Selectivity, reproducibility, stability and resistance analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra of Bi2O3, 4%, 5% and 6% N,S-CQD-Bi2O3, (b) XRD patterns of N,S-CQDs, Bi2O3, 4%, 5% and 6% N,S-CQD-Bi2O3.
FESEM of (a) Bi2O3, (b) 4% N,S-CQD-Bi2O3, (c) 5% N,S-CQD-Bi2O3, and (d) 6% N,S-CQD-Bi2O3.
TEM image of (a) Bi2O3, (b) N,S-CQDs (c) N,S-CQD- Bi2O3 (d) HR-TEM image of N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 (e) SAED pattern of N,S-CQD- Bi2O3, and (f) EDX analysis of N,S-CQD- Bi2O3.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of N,S-CQD-Bi2O3, deconvoluted XPS spectra of (b) Bi4f, (c) C1s, (d) O1s, (e) N1s, and (f) S2p.

(a) UV–vis DRS spectra of Bi2O3, CQD-Bi2O3, 4, 5, and 6 wt% N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 (b) Tauc plot of Bi2O3, CQD-Bi2O3, 4, 5, and 6 wt% N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 (c) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs, N,S-CQDs, Bi2O3, 4, 5%, and 6 wt% N,S-CQD-Bi2O3.
Rhodamine B degradation using (a) Bi2O3, (b) CQD-Bi2O3 and (c) 5% N,S-CQD-Bi2O3.
Degradation of (a) sunset yellow and (b) xylenol orange using 5% N,S-CQD-Bi2O3.
HPLC chromatogram of (a) tetracycline (b) 2,4-D, and (c) diuron using 5% N,S-CQD-Bi2O3.

(a) XRD and (b) FT-IR spectra of fresh and used catalyst (c) C/Co vs time graph of N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 for the degradation of Rh B, and (d) scavenger study (e) amperometric curves of N,S-CQD, Bi2O3 and 5% N,S-CQD-Bi2O3.

(a) CV runs of bare GCE, GCE/N,S-CQD, GCE/Bi2O3, GCE/N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 and GCE/N,S-CQD-Bi2O3/Aniline in PBS, (b) effect of pH, (c) effect of scan rate, (d) DPV of GCE/N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 with aniline concentrations from 5 to 30 nM in PBS solution, and (e) graph showing the concentration of aniline vs peak current.
(a) Chronoamperometric analysis, (b) stability, (c) repeatability, and (d) PEIS spectra of GCE/N,S-CQD-Bi2O3.
Schematic representation of the fabrication of the N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 nanocomposite.
Plausible photodegradation mechanism by 5% N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 photocatalyst.
Plausible mechanism of electrochemical sensing of aniline by GCE/N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 electrode.
Comparison of the present work with earlier published works.
A comparison of the current work to other recently published works.
Detection of aniline in real samples using DPV analysis (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and sulphur co-doped carbon quantum dot integrated bismuth oxide nanocomposite for photocatalytic degradation and electrochemical sensing applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, 686 560, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam-686 560, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study developed a novel nitrogen and sulphur co-doped carbon quantum dot-modified bismuth oxide binary composite (N,S-CQD- Bi2O3) through a hydrothermal method. The binary composite was successfully evaluated for its solar light-assisted photocatalytic abatement of toxic refractory pollutants such as dyes (rhodamine B, xylenol orange and sunset yellow), pesticides (2,4-D and diuron) and pharmaceutical drug (tetracycline). It was also successfully applied for the electrochemical detection of aniline molecules. The structural, morphological, optical, and electrochemical properties of the developed photocatalyst were investigated by XRD, FESEM, TEM, UV–vis DRS, XPS, PL, CV, amperometry and impedance techniques. Among the three different weight percentage binary composites, the 5% N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 displayed maximum photocatalytic performance. In all cases, the binary heterojunction exhibited a degradation efficiency of over 90% by converting the harmful compounds into less harmful ones. The scavenger experiments showed the roles of ˙OH and O2 −˙ radicals in the photodegradation. The improved photocatalytic behaviour was ascribed to the Z-scheme heterojunction formation, enhancements in visible light absorption, improved surface morphology and efficient separation and transfer of electron-hole pairs. The electrochemical detection method yields a LOD of 0.25 nM within the linear range of 5–35 nM. Real sample analysis of aniline also produces excellent results with outstanding reproducibility.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Surface and groundwater pollution caused by toxic organic dyes, hazardous pesticides, and pharmaceutical products from multiple sources severely threaten the environment, disrupting both animal and human life [1,2]. Removing these types of harmful chemicals from wastewater is imperative, yet the chemical stability and complex structure of these pollutants preclude them from degrading naturally [3]. The dyes such as rhodamine B (RhB), xylenol orange (XO), and sunset yellow (SY) are widely used in the textile and food industries. Without treatment, their direct discharge into the surroundings would contaminate natural water resources. Excessive use of organochlorine pesticide 2,4-Dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D) [4], organochlorin herbicide diuron [5] and the antibiotic tetracycline [6] increase their concentration in the soil and water sources. Aniline is a pivotal industrial raw material widely used in various fields, including printing and dyeing, rubber, pharmaceuticals, explosives, and pesticides [7].

All the mentioned chemicals exhibit adverse effects on terrestrial and aquatic life, including chromosomal mutations, toxic effects on the heart and central and peripheral nervous systems, carcinogenicity and acute pancreatitis [8]. Several advanced strategies, such as electro-oxidation, adsorption, biological treatment and photocatalysis, are frequently used to eliminate the contaminants from polluted water [9,10]. Among the strategies outlined, semiconductor photocatalysis is an effective, inexpensive, and sustainable method for minimising environmental pollution by degrading the pollutants into less hazardous materials by harnessing direct solar energy [11]. The photocatalytic activity of several semiconductor materials, such as ZnO, WO3, TiO2, and SnO2 etc., have been thoroughly investigated [12–16]. However, the wide band gap energy, faster recombination of active species, and requirement of UV light for the excitation limit their widespread practical application. Hence, the fabrication of photocatalysts exhibiting excitation in the visible light region is necessary to achieve maximum removal of pollutants from the waste water [17].

To detect the presence of aniline, several techniques have been developed, including surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [18], electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [19], liquid chromatography, and gas chromatography. However, the use of expensive reagents, complicated procedures, sophisticated instruments, the need for expert technicians, and the formation of numerous hazardous byproducts limit their practical applications. In recent years, electrochemistry methods have been widely used for the trace detection of numerous pollutants [20] due to their ease of operation, high sensitivity and selectivity, low reagent consumption, low detection limit, and low cost.

As a visible-light-responsive nanoparticle, the p-type semiconductor bismuth oxide (Bi2O3) has been widely used as a photocatalyst for pollutant decomposition and water splitting. They exhibited excellent properties such as good chemical and thermal stabilities, low toxicity, large surface areas, easy synthesis, and low cost. On the other hand, the rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriers generated in the system limits its widespread usage as an effective photocatalyst [21]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are zero-dimensional low-toxic nanomaterials, have recently been successfully incorporated into a number of semiconductor photoactive materials due to their low cost, photo-induced electron transfer behaviour, and the ability to produce up-converted photoluminescence [22,23]. The CQDs could improve the photodegradation activity of the conjugated parent photocatalyst, to which it is coupled by efficient charge separation, thereby suppressing the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers [24,25]. The band gap energy of most CQD-modified semiconductor photocatalysts is lowered since CQDs function well as electron harvesters and converters in the developed system [26].

In our work, we have modified Bi2O3 nanoparticles by nitrogen and sulphur co-doped CQDs (N,S-CQDs) to develop a N,S-CQD modified Bi2O3 binary composite (N,S-CQD-Bi2O3) through the hydrothermal method. Compared to unmodified Bi2O3, the developed heterogeneous catalyst exhibits improved photocatalytic activity under visible light. Here, N,S-CQDs are successfully integrated into the Bi2O3 crystal structure, facilitating efficient electron transfer and enhancing the number of active reaction carriers in the system. The most notable aspect of the work is the efficient use of visible light to degrade various refractory contaminants, including dyes, pesticides, and pharmaceutical products, within a short inter-well of time. In addition, we have developed a N,S-CQD doped bismuth oxide modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE/N,S-CQD-Bi2O3) to detect aniline precisely in environmental samples. The developed detector exhibits an excellent detection limit of 0.25 nM over a wide linear range of 5–35 nM. The sensor was also successful in detecting aniline in real samples.

Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), potassium bromide (KBr), o-Phenylenediamine (C₆H₄(NH₂)₂), methionine (C5H11NO2S), nitric acid (HNO3), rhodamine B (Rh B), xylenol orange (XO), sunset yellow (SY), 2,4-Dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D), tetracycline (TC), diuron and aniline. All chemicals were purchased from Merck, India.

N,S-CQDs were fabricated by dissolving 0.6 g o-phenylenediamine and 0.3 g methionine in 50 mL deionised water. The mixture was sonicated for 10 min to ensure homogeneous distribution of the reactants, followed by heating at 200 °C for 6 h in a Teflon-lined autoclave using an oven. The resultant brownish-yellow suspension was filtered for 24 h over a 0.22 μm dialysis membrane. The purified N,S-CQD solution was freeze-dried to yield powdered N,S-CQDs and which were then dissolved in distilled water to yield a 0.25 mg/mL N,S-CQD solution.

Bismuth nitrate (1.45 g) and potassium bromide (0.30 g) were dissolved in 10 mL dilute nitric acid, followed by the addition of 40 mL distilled water. The mixture was continuously stirred for 15 min and was heated at 260 °C for 8 h in a Teflon-lined autoclave. The yellow bismuth oxide powder nanoparticles were collected and characterised.

Bi2O3 (1 g) and N,S-CQD (0.05 g) was dissolved in about 20 mL of deionised water and sonicated for 10 min. The mixture was heated at 160 °C for 3 h, and the obtained powder was filtered and washed with deionised water to remove any impurities in the system. The powder is then washed three times with distilled ethanol, dried, and stored for further analysis. All the steps involved in the synthesis method are depicted in Scheme 1 .

The photodegradation activity of the composite was investigated by mixing approximately 0.025 g of the catalyst and 30 mL of dye solution (10 mg/L) or 2,4-D or diuron or tetracycline (20 mg/L) in a 100 ml beaker. The mixture was sonicated for 15 min and then magnetic stirring for 45 min in the dark to establish adsorption-desorption equilibrium. About 3 mL of the solution was initially collected before exposure to sunlight and labelled dark. After exposing the catalyst-dye mixture to sunlight, 3 mL of the solution was taken out at regular intervals. Later all the samples were centrifuged, and the absorbance of the samples was recorded using UV–vis. spectrophotometer.

The electrochemical studies were performed using a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as the working electrode; the counter electrode was a Pt wire, and an Ag/AgCl electrode was the reference electrode. All the electrochemical experiments were carried out using a N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 modified GCE electrode (GCE/N,S-CQD-Bi2O3) at pH 7.

The functional groups and types of bonds present in the developed composite were examined using FT-IR analysis. The FT-IR spectra of unmodified and modified Bi2O3 semiconductor nanoparticles is illustrated in Fig. 1 a. Unmodified Bi2O3 system displayed three bands at 520, 832 and 1015 cm−1, which were assigned to the characteristic vibrations of Bi–O bonds [27,28]. The modification of Bi2O3 by N,S-CQDs resulted in additional FT-IR bands at 1108, 1402, 1603,1710, and 3340 cm−1, representing symmetric stretching bands originating from the C–S, C–N, CC, CO, and O–H/N–H bonds, respectively [29]. The intensity of FT-IR bands obtained at 845 cm−1 in Bi2O3 was diminished in the N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 composite due to the interactions between the Bi–O bond of Bi2O3 and C–O group of N,S-CQDs. The structural and crystal behavior of the composite were further examined from the XRD analysis (Fig. 1b). In the XRD pattern the developed N,S-CQDs exhibited a broad peak at 2θ = 24.15°, which corresponds to the (002) planes of the graphitic plane structure and confirms the amorphous behaviour of developed N,S-CQDs [30]. The XRD pattern of Bi2O3 nanoparticles showed that they exhibited a monoclinic structure with the diffraction peaks located at 21.21, 24.15, 31.25, 33.26, 39.75, 44.58, 46.85, 50.25, 53.18, 56.21, 56.32, 61.05, 66.28, and 76.03° corresponding to the (102), (002), (121), (002), (131), (023), (223), (311), (113), (241), (222), (104), and (161) crystalline lattice planes, respectively. The peaks obtained agreed with the literature and JCPDS No. 71–2274 [31]. The successful addition of N,S-CQDs to the Bi2O3 system causes a sharpening of the XRD pattern at 24.15°, which corresponds to the (002) plane without significantly altering the other XRD patterns. This is due to the low concentration of N,S-CQDs in the synthesised binary composite.

FESEM and TEM analysis was performed to explore the structural and morphological features of the synthesised nanocomposite. The surface morphology of Bi2O3 is depicted in Fig. 2 a, which shows irregular-shaped non-uniform particles having an average diameter of around 36.25 nm. The morphology of the binary composite N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 with 4, 5, and 6 wt percent N,S-CQDs is given in Fig. 2b. The quantum dots were successfully anchored to the Bi2O3 nanoparticle system, as observed here. The TEM image of Bi2O3 (Fig. 3 a) further confirms the formation of non-uniform sized and irregular-shaped nanoparticles. The TEM images of N,S-CQDs demonstrated in Fig. 3b show that the N,S-CQDs are spherical with an average size of 6 nm. TEM image in Fig. 3c confirms that the small-sized N,S-CQDs are successfully wrapped on the surface of Bi2O3 in the N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 composite. The HR-TEM image in Fig. 3d supports the growth of N,S-CQDs on the surface of Bi2O3 with lattice fridges of 0.33 and 0.25 nm in Bi2O3 and N,S-CQDs, respectively. In the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of N,S-CQDs-Bi2O3, both rings and spots were observed (Fig. 3e) [32]. Braggs reflections from different planes produce different rings, and the bright spot stands for the crystalline nature. The composite is polycrystalline, as evidenced by the formation of several rings in the SAED Pattern. The elemental composition was examined by using EDX analysis which confirms the presence of elements carbon (C), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), sulphur (S) and bismuth (Bi) in the photocatalyst (Fig. 3f).

XPS analysis was performed to evaluate the chemical composition, elemental analysis, and chemical states of the photocatalyst. The XPS survey spectrum of N,S-CQDs-Bi2O3 (Fig. 4 a) displayed seven peaks corresponding to the elements bismuth (Bi 5d, 4f and 4p), sulphur (S2p), carbon (C1s), nitrogen (N1s) and oxygen (O1s). The deconvoluted XPS spectra of Bi(4f) shown in Fig. 4b exhibited two peaks at 159.19 and 164.52 eV, corresponding to the 4f7/2 and 4f5/2 states. The high-resolution spectra of C1s (Fig. 4c) revealed four peaks at 283.98, 284.56, 286.25, and 288.62 eV corresponding to the CC, C–N/C–S, C–O–Bi, and CO species, respectively. Since the oxygen atoms are exposed to four different chemical environments, the deconvoluted O1s spectrum exhibits four peaks corresponding to the Bi–O, CO, C–OH, OC–O species at 529.41, 530.04, 531.46 and 532.58 eV (Fig. 4d) [33]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 4e) was separated into two discrete peaks, each at 398.92, and 400.85 eV attributed to the C–N–C, and N–C/N–H bonds, respectively [34]. The deconvoluted S2p spectrum exhibits two peaks at 159.45 and 164.23 eV owing to the 2P3/2 and 2P1/2 states (Fig. 4f).

Diffused reflectance spectroscopic (DRS) studies were used to examine the photocatalytic ability of the designed catalyst (Fig. 5 a). Among pure Bi2O3, CQD-Bi2O3, and N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 composites having varying weight percentages of N,S-CQDs, the 5 wt% N,S-CQD- Bi2O3 system demonstrated the highest absorption characteristics near the visible region. The Kubelka-Munk equation [35] was used to measure the band gap energy of the composite.
(1) ∝hϑ = A(hϑ − Eg)1/n --------------

The absorption coefficient, Planck's constant, light frequency, proportionality constant, and band gap are expressed by the symbols α, h, ϑ, A, Eg, and n, respectively. The nature of the semiconductor determines the value of n; for indirect transitions, n = 2 and for direct transitions, n = 1/2. The values of Eg and n could be calculated from the ln (ahϑ) vs ln (hϑ - Eg) plot. The potential of the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) were determined using the equations,
(2) EVB = X - Ee + 0.5Eg --------------
(3) ECB = EVB - Eg --------------
Here EVB is the valence band potential, χ denotes the electronegativity of the material, Ee is the energy of free electron on the hydrogen scale, and Eg is the measured band gap of the developed catalyst [36]. Using Eqs. (2) and (3), the EVB calculated for Bi2O3 is 2.96 eV, and ECB is 0.49 eV. According to the results from the Tauc plot analysis (Fig. 5b), the bandgap of the Bi2O3 after modification with CQDs was reduced from 2.74 eV to 2.63 eV. Modifying Bi2O3 using N and S co-doped CQDs, further decreased the bandgap to 2.5 eV. The lower band gap energy of the N,S-CQDs-Bi2O3 confirms the excellent photocatalytic performance of the modified composite due to the availability of a greater number of active reactive species in the medium and their recombination propensity was significantly reduced by the action of N,S-CQDs [37].

The fluorescence spectra of CQD, N,S-CQD, Bi2O3, and different weight percent N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 in Fig. 5c demonstrate that the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CQD modified catalyst was lower than that of the unmodified Bi2O3, CQDs and N,S-CQDs revealing a reduced recombination tendency of electron-hole pairs. This enhances the availability of free electrons and holes in the reaction medium, leading to faster elimination of contaminants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of the N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 composite
ce:section.ce:para:
The photodegradation capacities of pure Bi2O3 and N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 composite, were evaluated using rhodamine B (Rh B), a common organic dye that pollutes water. The photocatalytic activity of pure Bi2O3, CQD-Bi2O3 and 5% N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 composite depicted in Fig. 6 a,b&c clearly shows that the activity of the Bi2O3 was significantly enhanced when modified with N,S-CQDs. Further confirmation of the improved activity was evident from the C/Co vs time graph shown in Fig. S1a. The kinetic studies of the reaction was followed from the –ln C/Co vs time plot (Fig. S1b), and the photocatalytic degradation reaction is assumed to follow pseudo-first-order kinetics with a rate constant of 0.329 min-1 according to the equation,
(4) -lnC/C0 = kt ----------------
where k and t represent the rate constant and time of the reaction [38].

Further photodegradation reactions of Rh B were carried out using N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 catalysts with varying N,S-CQD concentrations to optimise the composition of the catalyst. Fig. S2 a,b&c shows that the highest degradation activity was observed for 5 wt% composite than those with 4 and 6 wt% N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 composite. More photodegradation studies of the developed composite revealed that, similar to Rh B, the photocatalyst was highly effective in treating pollutants such as xylenol orange (XO), sunset yellow (SY), tetracycline, 2,4-D and diuron. The absorption spectra of SY and XO in Fig. 7 a&b showed that as the intensity of photoirradiation increases, the concentration of dyes in the reaction medium decreases progressively. Despite the fact that these harmful dyes undergo photodegradation with complete decolourisation, it is necessary to assess the degree of mineralisation of the degraded dyes. For that reason, total organic carbon (TOC) analysis was performed for all the dyes, both before and after degradation. The bar diagram of TOC analysis is shown in Fig. S3, indicating that Rh B, XO, and SY have respective TOC removal percentages of 96, 94, and 92%. The information demonstrates the quick and complete mineralisation and decolourisation of these dyes by the newly developed composite.

HPLC, mass, and TOC analysis were employed to monitor tetracycline, 2,4-D, and diuron degradation. The HPLC diagram of tetracycline exhibited a sharp peak at a retention time of 3 min (Fig. 8 a). The strength of the peak was rapidly reduced after 1 h of visible light irradiation, indicating the degradation of tetracycline into other smaller molecules. The HPLC analysis of untreated 2,4-D exhibited a sharp peak at a retention time of 5.4 min (Fig. 8b). The action of the photocatalyst on 2,4-D for a continuous period of 3 h resulted in the complete degradation of 2,4-D molecules into small less hazardous materials as evident from the HPLC diagram. The retention time of the diuron herbicide was about 5.84 min as evident from Fig. 8c. After 2 h of light irradiation, the photodegradation of the diuron resulted in the complete disappearance of the peak at 5.84 min.

High-resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS) analysis was performed to identify the molecular mass and chemical behaviour of the degradation products. The mass spectrum of pure tetracycline produced a sharp peak at m/z = 413.14, and after the degradation, the peak at 413.14 was completely removed, and a new peak appeared at m/z 179.03. The newly formed molecule is a bicyclo compound with a molecular formula C11H15O2, less hazardous than tetracycline (Fig. S4 a&b). The pesticide 2,4-D showed an intense peak at m/z = 218.95 and degraded into less toxic 2,4-Dichloro anisole with m/z of 176.95 (Fig. S5 a&b). Diuron herbicide having m/z = 231.05 was photodegraded into small, less harmful 3,4-Dichloro aniline with a m/z value of 162.05 (Fig. S6 a&b). The TOC analysis of the samples (Fig. S7) revealed that tetracycline, 2,4-D, and diuron had TOC removal of 90, 92, and 97%, respectively. This demonstrates almost complete degradation of pollutants in the reaction medium. A comparison of the photodegradation ability of the proposed sensor with some of the recently reported works is listed in Table 1 . The degradation time and efficiency of the N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 composite is superior to several previously reported results.

The most notable characteristics of the designed composite are its noteworthy stability and outstanding recyclability. The stability of the catalyst was examined using FT-IR and XRD analysis. The XRD pattern of fresh and used catalysts are nearly identical, as illustrated in Fig. 9 a. Furthermore, there is no discernible difference in their FT-IR spectra, as seen in Fig. 9b. The recyclability of the photocatalyst was investigated by running the photocatalytic degradation process for four consecutive cycles and observing the changes in the degradation tendency. The C/Co vs time graph in Fig. 9c shows that the catalyst activity remained nearly constant from the first to the last cycle, confirming the exceptional stability of the photocatalyst. These results confirm the future industrial applications of the developed catalyst as a solution to various environmental pollution.

Scavenger studies and electrochemical experiments were carried out to describe the enhanced activity of the 5% N,S-CQS-Bi2O3 catalyst and propose a plausible photodegradation mechanism. To find out the active species in the reaction medium responsible for the photodegradation, a scavenger study was performed using ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), benzoquinone (BQ) and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) as a scavenger for holes (h+), superoxide radical (.O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (.OH) respectively. The results shown in Fig. 9d revealed that the photodegradation ability of the active species decreases in the order .OH > .O2 − > h+. Hence, hydroxyl and superoxide radicals are the most active species responsible for photodegradation. Photocurrent measurements were achieved using the amperometric technique to evaluate the activity of the developed composite when exposed to visible light. The amperometric curve of N,S-CQDs, Bi2O3, and 5% N,S-CQDs-Bi2O3 composite in 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution irradiated with a xenon lamp (300 W) is illustrated in Fig. 9e. The value of photocurrent is highest for modified binary composite in comparison with unmodified N,S-CQDs and Bi2O3. The generation of free charge carriers in the system due to light irradiation enhances the photocurrent value [43].

According to the results of the scavenger study, hydroxyl (OH.) and superoxide (.O2 −) radicals are the most active species in the photodegradation of dyes, insecticides, and pharmaceutical products. The up-conversion characteristic and excellent light response of N,S-CQDs allow the 5% N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 composite to exhibit strong absorption in the visible light region. When exposed to sunlight, photogenerated holes and electrons are generated in the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) of Bi2O3. Because of the photo-induced electron migration property of CQDs, they can behave as electron donors as well as acceptors, preventing the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes and thus enhancing the photocatalytic activity of the nanocomposite. As a result, electrons excited from the valence band to the conduction band of Bi2O3 are transferred to the N,S-CQDs, leaving holes in the valence band and inhibiting electron-hole recombination. As a result, the excited electrons move rapidly and transfer into the conduction band of N,S-CQDs. Comparative photodegradation studies of CQD-Bi2O3 and 5% N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 reveal that doping with nitrogen and sulphur increases the activity of the photocatalyst. The function of N and S co-doping is to create quantum wells that serve as a residence for photogenerated electrons [44,45]. The generation of many quantum wells allows enhanced electron transfer between the CQDs and refractory pollutants. This permits efficient charge separation within the system and facilitates the formation of a large number of reactive species during the degradation process [46].

UV–vis DRS results also corroborate these findings. The high separation efficiency extends the lifetime of photogenerated charge carriers by preventing electron and hole recombination, leaving enough charge carriers to produce highly reactive species and significantly improving the photocatalytic activity of N,S-CQD-Bi2O3. The band edge potentials of N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 were determined, EVB and ECB were estimated to be 2.96 eV and 0.49 eV, respectively. Since the EVB of N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 is greater than the usual redox potential of .OH/OH− (1.99 eV vs NHE), hydroxyl radicals are the key active species in the degradation process. Photogenerated electrons on the surface of N,S-CQDs interact with adsorbed oxygen to form superoxide radical anions, which protonated to form highly reactive hydroxyl radicals. The holes in the system also produce hydroxyl radicals when they interact with adsorbed water molecules. These highly oxidising reactive species can degrade organic pollutants efficiently to less toxic compounds and finally give CO2 and water. When the composite was exposed to visible light, the electron-hole transfer process took place in the direction of Z; the process involved a Z-scheme mechanism. The schematic representation of the possible photodegradation mechanism is illustrated in Scheme 2 .
(5) Bi2O3 + hυ → Bi2O3(h+) + Bi2O3(e−) --------------
(6) Bi2O3 (e−)+ 5% N,S-CQDs → Bi2O3 + 5% N,S-CQD(e−) --------------
(7) 5% N,S-CQD(e−) → .O2 − + 5% N,S-CQDs --------------
(8) Bi2O3(h+) → OH. + Bi2O3 --------------

To extend the application of the developed composite, electrochemical studies were performed for the detection of aniline molecules. Cyclic voltammetric (CV) analysis was performed by using a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as the working electrode in a mixture of 5 mM[Fe(CN)6]3-/[Fe(CN)6]4 and 0.1 M KCl as the electrolyte. By drop casting N,S-CQDs, Bi2O3, and N,S-CQDs-Bi2O3 on the GCE surface, the cyclic voltammograms associated with these materials were recorded independently (Fig. 10 a). Both GCE/Bi2O3 and GCE/N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 exhibited well-defined oxidation and reduction peaks. The redox peak current was increased further by introducing 20 nM aniline solution into the GCE/N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 system, as indicated by the green line curve. This could be because the N,S-CQD modification increased the effective surface area of the electrode, resulting in an improved electron transfer process [47].

The pH optimisation studies were performed by using 20 nM aniline solutions to maximise the sensitivity of detection, and the maximum enhancement in the redox peak was observed at a pH of 4.5 (Fig. 10b). To understand the mechanism of sensing scan rate studies were performed from 10 to 100 mV/s at pH 7 in 0.1 M PBS. The intensity of the redox peaks was found to be enhanced o increasing the scan rate (Fig. 10c), suggesting that the process is reversible and that diffusion controls the electrochemical process [48]. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique was used to determine the detection limit (LOD) and the linear range of detection by changing the concentrations of aniline from 5 to 35 nM at the optimised conditions (Fig. 10d). A linear relationship was observed between peak height and the concentration of aniline. From the calibration curve obtained by plotting concentrations of aniline with the peak current, a straight line graph was observed with a R2 value of 0.99 (Fig. 10e). The detection limit was calculated by the equation,
(9) LOD = 3S/m --------------

S and m are the standard deviation and slope of the graph [49]. The LOD was found as 0.25 nM with a linear range of 5–35 nM. This astounding detection limit paved the way for the precise detection of trace amounts of aniline in aqueous solutions. The observable linear range accounts for future industrial applications and determining aniline concentration in real samples. Table 2 compares the sensing capabilities of previously reported electrochemical sensors with those of this newly developed sensor, confirming its high potential for detecting aniline.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility, stability and resistance analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Interfering molecules such as benzene, benzaldehyde, pyridine, N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), chloroform, phenol, acetone, Tetrahydrofuran (THF), and Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were used in the selectivity studies to determine the sensing efficiency of the composite. Chronoamperometric analysis was performed at various time intervals by adding 100 nM interfering molecules and 20 nM aniline solutions (Fig. 11 a). A well-observed current response was produced only by the aniline molecules, even in the presence of other analytes. The stability of the sensor was examined by keeping the modified electrode in a desiccator for one month, and only a negligible fluctuation in the current response was noticed (Fig. 11b). The reproducibility of the analysis were validated by continuously recording the CV response of the GCE/N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 for 40 cycles in a 20 nM aniline solution. There was no discernible difference in the area and position of the peaks even after 40 cycles (Fig. 11c), indicating that the experimental results are repeatable. The charge-carrying capacity and resistance of the developed sensor was investigated by using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The high resistance and low charge-carrying power of the GCE/N,S-CQDs and GCE/Bi2O3 were evident from their large radius semicircled Nyquist plots. The GCE/N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 exhibited lower resistance and higher conductivity than the unmodified N,S-CQDs and Bi2O3, as apparent from the Nyquist Plot with a low semicircular radius (Fig. 11d). This is due to the availability of higher number of free electrons and holes, allowing for fast electron transfer and excellent electrical conductivity in the modified system [53].

According to the literature, before the oxidation aniline undergoes a dimerisation process to generate p-aminodiphenylamine (ADPA) [54]. The formed ADPA dimer is adsorbed on the GCE/N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 surface during the electrochemical reaction and fast redox reactions occurs. The oxidation of ADPA occurs with the transfer of two proton and two electrons to the GCE/N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 surface. During oxidation, the hydrogen atoms present on the two amine groups on the ADPA are removed. The reduction process involves the absorption of two proton and two electrons from the electrode surface by the oxidised ADPA molecules. Scheme 3 depicts a possible mechanism for the electrochemical detection of aniline by the GCE/N,S-CQD-Bi2O3.

Some foods, textiles, and water sources contained trace amounts of aniline. Real sample analysis was carried out in tap and river water samples using the developed electrode following the DPV method. The recovery results for the collected water samples spiked with known amounts of aniline are shown in Table 3 . The proposed methods yielded excellent recoveries ranging from 99.1 to 102.1%. These findings demonstrate that the proposed method is reliable and accurate for identifying aniline in the environment.

In summary, the hydrothermal method was successfully used to fabricate a novel, highly efficient, and reusable N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 photocatalyst. Maximal catalytic efficiency was exhibited by 5% N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 with a band gap of 2.47 eV. The improved photocatalytic behaviour was due to the enhanced surface area, increased visible light absorption power, and reduced electron-hole recombination by incorporating N,S-CQDs into the Bi2O3 nanocrystals. The photodegradation process follows pseudo-first order kinetics with a rate constant of 0.329 min−1. Under sunlight, the as-synthesised catalyst degraded 96% of Rh B, 94% of XO, and 92% of SY in 12 min. The TOC removal of TC was 90% in 1 h, 2,4-D was 92% in 3 h, and diuron was 87% in 2 h. According to scavenger studies, photogenerated holes and electrons initiate the degradation process and the hydroxyl radicals generated from holes play the most crucial role in the degradation process. The degradation products were identified using HPLC and mass analysis and were found to be less toxic than their parent compounds. Recognising these compounds after degradation and other morphological, optical, and electrochemical characterisations helps to propose a plausible Z-scheme mechanism. In addition, a sensitive and selective GCE/N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 electrochemical sensor was developed to detect trace amounts of aniline. The modified electrode exhibits a linear response towards aniline over the linear range of 0–35 nM with a LOD of 0.25 nM. The proposed method was successfully applied for the determination of aniline in a tap and river water sample with excellent accuracy and reproducibility. In future, the proposed nanocomposite would have excellent applications in sewage treatment and detection of toxic chemicals at the industrial level.
Bony K. John: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – original draft. Beena Mathew: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Bony K John would like to acknowledge the financial support from the UGC India - Junior Research Fellowship (grant number BININ01764209).
